entry.title: SBPA Desktop Automation -- How to select a specific value from ARIBA DDM dropdown(bh=DDM) at runtime
entry.content:
Environment:

  • SAP Build Process Automation (Cloud Studio)
  • Desktop Agent, ARIBA framework application
  • Browser: Microsoft Edge
  • Target: SAP Ariba Log Settings page

Problem:

I need to programmatically select a specific value from an ARIBA DDM dropdown (bh=DDM, role=combobox) during a bot automation. The value to select changes dynamically per loop iteration (node name from a NodeItem loop variable).

The dropdown contains 342 items and uses virtual rendering -- items are only loaded into the DOM when the dropdown is open and filtered by typing.

What I have declared:

  • e_logicalNode -- the DDM element (DIV bh=DDM role=combobox)
  • e_allDDI -- collection of all DDI items (bh=DDI role=option, 342 elements)

What I have tried:

Attempt 1 -- Click DDM + Keystroke to filter + Click e_allDDI[0]:

  • Click e_logicalNode opens the dropdown
  • Keystroke with expression editor types the node name (confirmed resolving correctly via Log Message)
  • Click e_allDDI[0] always clicks the first item in the full unfiltered collection, not the filtered result

Attempt 2 -- Click DDM + Keystroke + {Return}:

  • Click e_logicalNode opens the dropdown
  • Keystroke {Ctrl}A + {Delete} to clear filter, then type node name, then {Return}
  • Dropdown opens but Keystroke does not interact with the filter -- default node remains selected
  • Root cause suspected: clicking the DDM div opens the popup but focus does NOT land on the filter input inside the popup

Attempt 3 -- Script Element (WEB framework):

  • Created a WEB framework application and used Script Element activity to execute JavaScript
  • All JavaScript approaches fail: document, window, and element are all undefined in the Script Element content context
  • Error: SCRIPTITEM failed in content context: Cannot read properties of undefined

Confirmed facts:

  • DDI items ARE in the DOM even when dropdown is closed (document.querySelector('[awname*=C1_UI1]') found via browser console)
  • mousedown + mouseup + click events on a DDI item via browser console DO select it correctly
  • The filter input inside the DDM popup is a separate element that requires explicit focus before typing

Question:

  1. How do I correctly select a specific value from an ARIBA DDM dropdown at runtime in SBPA Desktop Automation?
  2. Is there a native SBPA activity for ARIBA DDM value selection (similar to SAPUI5 Combo Box Set Value activity)?
  3. If Keystroke is the correct approach, how do I ensure focus lands on the filter input inside the DDM popup after clicking the DDM?

entry.title: AI Agent Evaluation Is the Most Important Skill in AI Engineering - Here's How to Do It
entry.content:
Blog 4 of N: Is Your Agent Actually Working? Building AI Agents Is Easy. Evaluating Them Is the Real Skill - Here's How to Do It

What Are We Building?

In Blog 2, you built a sales analyst agent with tools, structured output, memory, and streaming. In Blog 3, you wrapped it in a FastAPI server with a chat UI.

It works. You ask "How were sales in January?" and get a nicely formatted answer. Ship it?

Not so fast. How do you know it's actually correct?

You tried it a few times. The answers looked reasonable. So you deployed. That's how most AI agents go to production today - vibes-based testing. And it's a problem, because agents fail silently:

What goes wrongWhat the user sees
Agent calls the wrong toolA confident answer built on wrong data
Right tool, wrong arguments - fetches March instead of JanuaryCorrect-looking numbers from the wrong month
Tool returns perfect data, agent hallucinates numbers"Revenue was $5,000" when the real number is $1,159
User asks "What's the weather?" - agent answers anywayA made-up response with no data source
Model update on SAP AI Core changes behaviorYesterday's correct answers become today's wrong ones

Notice the pattern: none of these throw an error. The agent doesn't crash. It returns a polished, well-formatted, completely wrong response. A broken agent looks exactly like a working one.

This is why evaluation is the most important skill in AI engineering.

You can build the fanciest agent - best tools, cleverest prompt, latest model - but without systematic evaluation, you're flying blind. Traditional software has unit tests, integration tests, CI/CD pipelines. Nobody ships a web app by clicking around and saying "looks fine." AI agents deserve the same rigor. They just need different tools.

If this shift in thinking resonates with you, read Agent-Driven Development: Why Software Engineering Needs a New Mental Model - it covers why debugging agents means analyzing traces, not scanning logs, and why evals replace unit tests in the agent world.

In this blog, we'll put that thinking into practice. We'll evaluate the sales agent from Blog 2, step by step. The concepts - tracing, scorers, golden trajectories, LLM-as-Judge - apply to any agent you build, not just this one. A customer support agent, a document analysis agent, a code review agent - the evaluation pattern is the same.

We'll use MLflow for tracing and evaluation. It's open-source, integrates with LangChain in one line, and gives you a dashboard to inspect traces and compare evaluation runs. If you prefer other tools, the same concepts apply - LangSmith, Arize Phoenix, Braintrust, and Weights & Biases Weave all offer similar tracing and evaluation capabilities. The scorers and patterns you learn here are framework-agnostic.

We'll build this incrementally. Each section teaches a concept (theory), then you apply it (lab). By the end, you'll have a repeatable evaluation pipeline that catches regressions before your users do.

Prerequisites

  • Completed Blog 2 - you have the first-react-agent project with the working sales agent
  • The agent runs - you can ask "How were sales in January?" and get a response
  • No new SAP AI Core setup - same service key, same deployments

Project root: All commands in this blog assume you are in the first-react-agent project root (the folder containing pyproject.toml and sales.csv) unless stated otherwise. File paths like eval/scorers.py or src/first_react_agent/agent.py are relative to this root.

Windows users: Most commands (uv, python, uvx) work the same on both macOS and Windows. Where shell commands differ (folder creation), both versions are shown.

Step 1: Why Agent Evaluation is Different

Theory

Testing a traditional API is simple. You send an input, get an output, compare it to the expected result:

Traditional API:
  input: "2 + 2"  →  output: "4"  →  assert output == "4"  ✓

An agent doesn't work this way. When you ask "How were sales in January?", the agent executes a chain of decisions:

User question: "How were sales in January?"
  → LLM reads the question
  → LLM decides which tool to call          (tool selection)
  → LLM constructs the arguments             (parameter accuracy)
  → Tool executes and returns data            (tool execution)
  → LLM reads the returned data
  → LLM writes a human-readable answer       (synthesis)

Each step can fail independently:

 

StepWhat can go wrongExample
Tool selectionCalls the wrong toolCalls calculate_sales_summary before fetching data
Parameter accuracyPasses wrong argumentsSends month="Jan" instead of month="January"
Tool executionTool returns an errorCSV file not found
SynthesisHallucinates numbersSays "revenue was $5,000" when tool returned $1,159
SynthesisIgnores dataSkips the top customer from the summary
ScopeAnswers off-topic questionsResponds to "What's the weather?" with made-up data

You can't evaluate an agent with a single metric. You need to check each step of the chain.

There's another problem: agents are non-deterministic. Ask the same question three times, you'll get three different wordings. The structure might differ. The phrasing will differ. But the facts should be the same. This means assert response == "expected string" will never work reliably.

Lab

Run your agent three times with the exact same question. You'll see why traditional testing breaks down.

Open a terminal and navigate to the project root:

cd first-react-agent
uv run agent

Ask "How were sales in January?" three times (start a new conversation each time by restarting the agent with Ctrl+C and uv run agent). Notice:

  • The wordings might change
  • The structure might differ (bullet points vs paragraphs vs tables)
  • But the numbers should be consistent (same total orders, same revenue, same top customer)

This is the core challenge: the facts must be stable even when the words aren't.

Step 2: The 4 Layers of Agent Evaluation

Theory

Think of agent evaluation as 4 concentric layers, from innermost (cheapest, most deterministic) to outermost (most expensive, most subjective) :

Layer 1: Unit Tests - Does each tool work in isolation? Does get_monthly_sales("January", 2025) return the right rows from the CSV? Does calculate_sales_summary compute the right totals? These are plain Python tests. No LLM involved. If your tools return wrong data, nothing above this layer matters.

Layer 2: Behavioral Assertions - With a real LLM in the loop, does the agent behave correctly? Did it call the right tool? Did it pass the right arguments? Are the numbers in the response grounded in the tool's output? These are still deterministic Python checks - no LLM judge needed.

Layer 3: End-to-End Trajectories - Full scenario tests. A "golden trajectory" defines: the user's question, which tools should be called in what order, what parameters should be passed, and what facts should appear in the response. This is your agent's regression test suite.

Layer 4: Subjective Quality - Some things can't be checked with code. "Was the answer clear?" "Did it fully explain the data?" That's where you use an LLM to evaluate the agent's output - called "LLM-as-Judge."

The key insight: build from the inside out. If Layer 1 is broken (tools return wrong data), no amount of Layer 4 evaluation will help. Fix the foundation first.

In this blog, we'll focus on Layers 2-4. Layer 1 is standard unit testing - you already know how to do that. The interesting challenge is evaluating agent behavior with a real LLM.

Step 3: Setup - MLflow Tracing

Theory

Before you can evaluate agent behavior, you need to see agent behavior. When the agent answers a question, what happened inside? Which tools did it call? What arguments did it pass? What did the tools return? How long did each step take?

This is what tracing does. A trace captures the full execution of an agent invocation as a tree of spans - one span for each step (LLM call, tool call, etc.).

Trace: "How were sales in January?"
├── LLM Call (span)
│   input: [system prompt + user message]
│   output: "I'll call get_monthly_sales"
│   tool_calls: [{name: "get_monthly_sales", args: {month: "January", year: 2025}}]
│
├── Tool: get_monthly_sales (span)
│   input: {month: "January", year: 2025}
│   output: "order_id,date,customer,product,quantity,unit_price\n..."
│
├── LLM Call (span)
│   input: [previous messages + tool result]
│   output: "I'll calculate the summary"
│   tool_calls: [{name: "calculate_sales_summary", args: {sales_data: "..."}}]
│
├── Tool: calculate_sales_summary (span)
│   input: {sales_data: "..."}
│   output: "Total Orders: 4\nTotal Revenue: $1,159.62\n..."
│
└── LLM Call (span)
    input: [previous messages + tool result]
    output: "Based on January 2025 sales data, here are the results..."

Each span records its inputs, outputs, timing, and metadata. This gives you full visibility into the agent's decision chain.

Why MLflow?

MLflow is an open-source platform for managing the ML lifecycle. For LLM applications, it provides three things we need:

  1. Tracing - auto-captures every LangChain/LangGraph agent invocation as a span tree. One line of code: mlflow.langchain.autolog(). No manual instrumentation needed.
  2. Evaluation - mlflow.genai.evaluate() runs your agent against a dataset and applies scorers (quality checks) to every response. Results are stored, compared across runs, and visible in a dashboard.
  3. UI - a web dashboard where you can inspect traces, view evaluation results, and compare runs side by side.

The alternative is building all of this yourself: writing trace capture code, building a results database, creating a dashboard. MLflow gives you this out of the box.

Auto-tracing is the key feature for us. From the MLflow LangChain docs: calling mlflow.langchain.autolog() patches LangChain's core methods (invoke, stream, batch, etc.). On every invocation, a tracer callback is injected that captures the full execution graph - one span per component. No code changes to your agent needed.

Lab

Add MLflow to your project:

Run this from the project root (first-react-agent/) :

uv add "mlflow[genai]"

We use mlflow[genai] because we need both tracing and the mlflow.genai.evaluate() API. The [genai] extra includes the evaluation framework.

Once it finishes, verify the installation:

uv run python -c "import mlflow; print(mlflow.__version__)"

You should see a version number like 3.x.x. If you see an import error, run uv add "mlflow[genai]" again.

Add MLflow settings to your configuration:

In Blog 1, we used a .env file with pydantic-settings to manage SAP AI Core credentials. We follow the same pattern for MLflow. Open src/first_react_agent/config.py (path from project root) and add the MLflow fields to your existing AICoreConfig class:

from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict


class AICoreConfig(BaseSettings):
	model_config = SettingsConfigDict(
		env_file=".env",
		env_file_encoding="utf-8",
	)
	
	aicore_auth_url: str
	aicore_client_id: str
	aicore_client_secret: str
	aicore_resource_group: str = "default"
	aicore_base_url: str
	aicore_model: str
	llm_max_output_tokens: int = 4096
	
	# MLflow settings
	mlflow_enabled: bool = True
	mlflow_tracking_uri: str = "http://127.0.0.1:5000"
	mlflow_experiment_name: str = "sales-agent-eval"

Three new fields, all with sensible defaults. mlflow_enabled lets you turn tracing on and off without changing code.

Now add these values to your .env file (in the project root, alongside pyproject.toml). Add these lines below the existing SAP AI Core settings:

AICORE_AUTH_URL=https://xx-xx-xx-xx.xx.xx.xx.ondemand.com
AICORE_BASE_URL=https://api.ai.xx.xx-xx.xx.xx.xx.ondemand.com
AICORE_CLIENT_ID=<client_id>
AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET=<secret>
AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP=<rg>
AICORE_MODELS=gpt-5
LLM_MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS=4096

MLFLOW_ENABLED=true
MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI=http://127.0.0.1:5000
MLFLOW_EXPERIMENT_NAME=sales-agent-eval

Why 127.0.0.1 and not localhost? The MLflow server binds to 127.0.0.1 by default (you'll see it in the startup logs). Using the exact same address avoids DNS resolution issues on some systems.

Enable auto-tracing and configure MLflow in your agent:

Open src/first_react_agent/agent.py (path from project root). We configure MLflow inside create_app(), not at module level. This way the agent only connects to the MLflow server when it's actually created. If the server isn't running and you set MLFLOW_ENABLED=false, uv run agent still works fine.

Your updated agent.py (full file at src/first_react_agent/agent.py) :

import mlflow
from langchain.agents import create_agent
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import InMemorySaver

from first_react_agent.client import create_llm
from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig
from first_react_agent.tools import get_monthly_sales, calculate_sales_summary


def create_app():
    config = AICoreConfig()

    if config.mlflow_enabled:
        mlflow.langchain.autolog()
        mlflow.set_tracking_uri(config.mlflow_tracking_uri)
        mlflow.set_experiment(config.mlflow_experiment_name)

    llm = create_llm(config)

    agent = create_agent(
        llm,
        tools=[get_monthly_sales, calculate_sales_summary],
        system_prompt=(
            "You are a sales analyst assistant. "
            "The sales data covers the year 2025. If the user does not specify a year, assume 2025. "
            "When a user asks about sales for a specific month, first use get_monthly_sales to fetch the raw data, "
            "then pass that data to calculate_sales_summary to compute the statistics. "
            "Always report the summary back to the user. "
            "If the user asks something unrelated to sales data, politely decline and explain that you can only assist with sales queries."
        ),
        checkpointer=InMemorySaver(),
    )
    return agent

What changed from Blog 2:

ChangeWhy
import mlflowMLflow tracing library
config.mlflow_enabled checkOnly set up tracing if enabled in .env
mlflow.langchain.autolog()Patches LangChain methods to auto-capture traces
mlflow.set_tracking_uri(...)Tells MLflow where to send traces (from .env)
mlflow.set_experiment(...)Groups traces under a named experiment (from .env)

Everything reads from config. No hardcoded URLs. No env vars passed on the command line.

Start the MLflow tracking server:

Open a separate terminal, navigate to the project root, and start the MLflow server:

you should be inside first-react-agent folder to run the below command

uvx mlflow server

Wait until you see this line - it means the server is ready:

INFO:     Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:5000 (Press CTRL+C to quit)

Once you see it, open http://127.0.0.1:5000 in your browser. You'll see the MLflow UI with an empty experiment list. Keep this terminal running - the server needs to stay up for the rest of the blog.

Run the agent and inspect the trace:

Go back to your first terminal (not the one running the MLflow server) and run the agent from the project root:

uv run agent

No extra environment variables needed - everything is read from .env.

Ask "How were sales in January?" - then go to the MLflow UI at http://127.0.0.1:5000. Click on the "sales-agent-eval" experiment. You'll see a trace with the question as its title.

Click into the trace. You'll see the full execution chain - every LLM call and tool call listed as collapsible spans:

How were sales in January 2025?
├── LLM was called                          1.47s
├── get_monthly_sales was called            8.77ms
├── LLM was called                          2.05s
├── calculate_sales_summary was called      0.56ms
└── LLM was called                          1.78s

That's three LLM calls and two tool calls - exactly the ReAct pattern from Step 1. The agent reasoned, called a tool, reasoned again, called another tool, then synthesized the final answer.

What to look for in each span:

  • LLM spans (labeled with your model's provider name, e.g., "ChatOpenAI was called") - expand the Inputs section to see the system prompt and user message. Expand Outputs to see the model's response, including tool_calls with the tool name and arguments it chose (e.g., "name": "get_monthly_sales", "month": "January", "year": 2025)
  • Tool spans (labeled "get_monthly_sales was called" or "calculate_sales_summary was called") - expand Inputs to see the exact arguments the agent passed (e.g., month: "January", year: 2025). Expand Outputs to see the raw data returned (the CSV rows from sales.csv)
  • Latency - each span shows its execution time on the right. LLM calls take seconds (network round-trip to SAP AI Core), while tool calls take milliseconds (local Python execution). Total latency is shown at the top of the trace

At the bottom of the trace, you'll find the Outputs section with the full message history - every message exchanged between the user, the model, and the tools, in order. This is the complete conversation that produced the final answer.

This visibility is what makes systematic evaluation possible. Without traces, you only see the final answer. With traces, you see every decision the agent made to arrive at that answer.

Layer 2: Behavioral Assertions - Steps 4, 5, and 6 cover Layer 2 from our evaluation model. These are deterministic Python checks that verify agent behavior with a real LLM in the loop. No LLM judge needed - just code.

Step 4: Your First Scorer - Tool Selection

Theory

Now that you can see inside your agent (traces), you can start checking its behavior. The most fundamental check is tool selection: did the agent call the right tool for the question?

If the agent picks the wrong tool, everything downstream is wrong - even if the final answer looks plausible. For example, if the user asks "How were sales in January?" and the agent calls calculate_sales_summary without first calling get_monthly_sales, it has no data to summarize. The wrong tool at step 1 cascades into a wrong answer at the end.

In MLflow, a quality check is called a scorer. From the MLflow scorers docs: a scorer is a function that receives trace data and returns a quality assessment. Scorers come in two flavors:

  1. Code-based scorers - pure Python functions. Deterministic, fast, cheap. Perfect for checks like "did it call the right tool?"
  2. LLM-powered judges - use an LLM to evaluate subjective quality. Slower, more expensive, non-deterministic. Use for "was the answer clear?"

We start with code-based scorers because they're reliable and free. We'll add LLM judges later in Step 8.

From the MLflow custom scorers docs: you create a custom scorer using the @scorer decorator. The function can accept any combination of these parameters (MLflow injects them by name) :

  • inputs - the question sent to the agent
  • outputs - the agent's final response
  • expectations - ground truth (what you expect)
  • trace - the full MLflow trace with all spans

The function can return a bool, int, float, str, or a Feedback object for richer results.

Lab

Create the evaluation directory inside the project root (first-react-agent/) :

macOS / Linux:

mkdir -p eval
touch eval/__init__.py

Windows (PowerShell) :

New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path eval -Force
New-Item -ItemType File -Path eval/__init__.py -Force

Your project should now have this new folder alongside the existing src/ folder:

first-react-agent/
├── src/first_react_agent/   # Your agent code (from Blog 2)
├── eval/                    # NEW - evaluation suite
│   └── __init__.py
├── pyproject.toml
└── sales.csv

Create eval/scorers.py (path from project root) with your first scorer:

from mlflow.entities import Feedback
from mlflow.genai.scorers import scorer


def get_tool_calls(trace):
    """Extract tool names from assistant messages in the trace."""
    tools = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "assistant":
                    for tc in msg.get("tool_calls", []):
                        name = tc.get("function", {}).get("name")
                        if name:
                            tools.append(name)
    return tools


@scorer
def tool_selection(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check that the agent called the expected tools."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # If no tools expected (e.g., off-topic question), check none were called
    if not expected_tools:
        if not actual_tools:
            return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly called no tools.")
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Expected no tool calls, but called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check each expected tool was called
    missing = [t for t in expected_tools if t not in actual_tools]
    if missing:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Missing tools: {missing}. Called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    return Feedback(value=True, rationale=f"All expected tools called: {actual_tools}")

How does the scorer find tool calls?

When evaluate() traces the agent, each trace contains the full conversation between the LLM and the tools. The assistant messages include a tool_calls field listing which tools were called. The get_tool_calls helper reads these messages and extracts the tool names.

Why parse messages instead of using trace.search_spans(span_type=SpanType.TOOL)? Some LLM providers create dedicated TOOL spans in MLflow traces - one span per tool call, with the tool name, inputs, and outputs neatly separated. MLflow's official examples (using OpenAI Agents SDK) work this way. But not all providers do. The SAP AI Core LangChain integration produces a single ChatCompletions span containing the entire conversation - tool calls are embedded inside the messages, not in separate spans. The get_tool_calls helper handles this by reading the conversation messages directly. This approach works regardless of whether your provider creates TOOL spans or not.

This scorer:

  1. Extracts tool names from the assistant messages in the trace using get_tool_calls(trace)
  2. Compares the actual tools called against the expected tools from the test case
  3. Returns a Feedback object with a boolean value and a human-readable rationale

Did you know? MLflow provides built-in ToolCallCorrectness and ToolCallEfficiency scorers. These are LLM-as-Judge scorers that analyze traces to check tool usage. They work best when your provider creates TOOL spans. Writing your own deterministic scorer (as we did above) gives you a free, instant check that works with any trace format. In production, you might combine both: your custom scorer for fast CI checks, and the built-in scorers for deeper LLM-based analysis in nightly runs.

Now create eval/dataset.py (path from project root) with test cases:

SALES_AGENT_DATASET = [
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "How were sales in January?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
        },
    },
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "What's the weather in Berlin?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": [],
        },
    },
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Give me a complete summary of March sales"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
        },
    },
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "What were sales in February 2025?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
        },
    },
]

Each test case has inputs (what to ask the agent) and expectations (what we expect). The inputs keys must match the parameters of the prediction function we write next.

Now create eval/evaluate.py (path from project root) - the main evaluation runner.

This file has three sections: setup, predict function, and run evaluation. Let's look at the complete file first, then walk through each section:

"""Run evaluation suite for the sales agent."""

import os
os.environ["MLFLOW_ENABLED"] = "false"

import mlflow
from uuid import uuid4

from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig
from first_react_agent.agent import create_app
from eval.scorers import tool_selection
from eval.dataset import SALES_AGENT_DATASET

# Configure MLflow tracking (same server as tracing)
config = AICoreConfig()
mlflow.set_tracking_uri(config.mlflow_tracking_uri)
mlflow.set_experiment(config.mlflow_experiment_name)

agent = create_app()

# Fix tracing for evaluate()'s thread pool (see explanation below)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(log_traces=True, run_tracer_inline=True)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(disable=True)


def predict_fn(question: str) -> str:
    """Run the agent and return the final response text."""
    result = agent.invoke(
        {"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": question}]},
        {"configurable": {"thread_id": str(uuid4())}},
    )
    return result["messages"][-1].content


results = mlflow.genai.evaluate(
    data=SALES_AGENT_DATASET,
    predict_fn=predict_fn,
    scorers=[tool_selection],
)

print(results.tables["eval_results"])

What does evaluate() do?

From the MLflow evaluate() docs: mlflow.genai.evaluate() takes three things:

  1. data - your test cases. A list of dicts with inputs and expectations.
  2. predict_fn - a function that takes the inputs and returns the agent's response. MLflow calls this once per test case and automatically captures a trace.
  3. scorers - a list of scorer functions to run on each result. Each scorer receives the inputs, outputs, expectations, and the captured trace.

That is it. MLflow handles the loop: for each test case, call predict_fn, capture a trace, run every scorer, collect the results.

Why the setup lines?

The setup block (lines before predict_fn) solves two problems that come from running our agent inside evaluate() instead of running it directly. Here is what each part does:

1. os.environ["MLFLOW_ENABLED"] = "false" (before imports)

When you run the agent normally (uv run agent), create_app() enables MLflow tracing via autolog(). That is great for normal use. But evaluate() needs to control tracing itself - it wraps each predict_fn call in its own trace so scorers can inspect what happened.

If both create_app() and evaluate() try to manage tracing, they conflict. The fix: set the environment variable to "false" before importing create_app. Since our config class reads from environment variables (which take priority over .env values), create_app() sees MLFLOW_ENABLED=false and skips its own tracing setup. This lets evaluate() handle tracing without interference.

2. The autolog enable-then-disable pair

mlflow.langchain.autolog(log_traces=True, run_tracer_inline=True)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(disable=True)

This looks odd, but it solves a threading issue. evaluate() runs your predict_fn in background threads for performance. By default, LangChain's tracer also runs in a separate background thread. When both use separate threads, the trace data gets lost - you see a NonRecordingSpan error and your scorers crash because they have no trace to inspect.

The fix: run_tracer_inline=True tells LangChain's tracer to run in the same thread as your agent. We enable autolog to save this setting, then immediately disable it. When evaluate() later re-enables autolog internally, it picks up the saved run_tracer_inline=True setting and tracing works correctly.

You do not need to change these setup lines. They are the same in every evaluate.py - the only thing that changes between steps is the scorer imports and the scorers list.

Run the evaluation:

Make sure your MLflow server is still running in the separate terminal (from Step 3). In your main terminal, run from the project root (first-react-agent/) :

uv run python -m eval.evaluate

You will see something like below

View the evaluation results at http://127.0.0.1:5000/#/experiments/1/evaluation-runs?selectedRunUuid=***********

Copy and Open the url in a browser. You will see the evaluation runs page with two panels:

Left panel: Run summary. Your run shows tool_selection = 1 (meaning 100% pass rate).

Right panel: Trace-level detail. You will see two types of traces: evaluation traces (one per scenario in the dataset) and duplicate traces from autolog (harmless noise - you can ignore these). The evaluation traces show:

  • Request and Response - the question and the agent's answer
  • Tokens and Execution time - cost and latency per scenario
  • tool_selection - True for evaluation traces, null for autolog duplicates
  • expected_tools - the tool list from the dataset, so you can verify at a glance

Click any evaluation trace row to inspect the full execution - every LLM call, tool call, input, and output, just like the traces from Step 3.

Step 5: Groundedness - Are the Numbers Real?

Theory

For a data-driven agent, this is the most critical check. When the agent says "Total Revenue: $1,159.62" - does that number actually exist in the tool's output? Or did the LLM make it up?

This matters because users act on the numbers your agent reports. If an analyst reads "January revenue was $5,000" and the real number is $1,159, they make wrong decisions. In any data application - sales, finance, analytics - every number must trace back to source data.

Groundedness means: the numbers in the agent's response come from the tool's output, not from the LLM's imagination. No invented numbers. No hallucination.

This is a deterministic check - pure Python, no LLM judge needed. You extract numbers from the tool output, extract numbers from the response, and verify that the response numbers overlap with the tool output numbers. If the agent mentions numbers that have zero overlap with what the tools returned, it is hallucinating.

Why this matters before production: Hallucinated numbers are the most dangerous type of agent failure. The agent sounds confident, the response looks correct, but the data is wrong. A groundedness check catches these failures automatically. Run it before every deployment, and you will never ship an agent that invents numbers.

Lab

Open eval/scorers.py (path from project root) and add the groundedness scorer. Here is the complete file with both scorers:

"""Evaluation scorers for the sales agent."""

import re

from mlflow.entities import Feedback
from mlflow.genai.scorers import scorer


def extract_numbers(text: str) -> set[str]:
    """Extract all numbers from text, including decimals and comma-separated."""
    numbers = set()
    text = str(text)
    # Pass 1: treat commas as thousand separators (e.g., "$1,799.46")
    for match in re.findall(r"\d[\d,]*\.\d+|\d[\d,]*", text):
        numbers.add(match.replace(",", ""))
    # Pass 2: split by commas first, then extract (handles CSV like "4,129.99")
    for field in text.replace("\n", ",").split(","):
        for match in re.findall(r"\d+\.\d+|\d+", field.strip()):
            numbers.add(match)
    return numbers


def get_tool_calls(trace):
    """Extract tool names from assistant messages in the trace."""
    tools = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "assistant":
                    for tc in msg.get("tool_calls", []):
                        name = tc.get("function", {}).get("name")
                        if name:
                            tools.append(name)
    return tools


def get_tool_outputs(trace):
    """Extract tool output text from tool messages in the trace."""
    outputs = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "tool":
                    outputs.append(msg.get("content", ""))
    return outputs


@scorer
def tool_selection(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check that the agent called the expected tools."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # If no tools expected (e.g., off-topic question), check none were called
    if not expected_tools:
        if not actual_tools:
            return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly called no tools.")
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Expected no tool calls, but called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check each expected tool was called
    missing = [t for t in expected_tools if t not in actual_tools]
    if missing:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Missing tools: {missing}. Called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    return Feedback(value=True, rationale=f"All expected tools called: {actual_tools}")


@scorer
def groundedness(inputs, outputs, trace):
    """Response numbers should come from tool output, not be hallucinated."""
    tool_outputs = get_tool_outputs(trace)

    if not tool_outputs:
        # No tools called - nothing to ground against
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No tool calls to check against.")

    # Collect all numbers from tool outputs
    tool_numbers = set()
    for output in tool_outputs:
        tool_numbers.update(extract_numbers(output))

    # Extract numbers from the agent's response
    response_numbers = extract_numbers(str(outputs))

    if not response_numbers:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No numbers in response to verify.")

    # Check how many response numbers are grounded in tool output
    grounded = response_numbers & tool_numbers

    if not grounded:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale="No response numbers found in tool output - possible hallucination.",
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"{len(grounded)} of {len(response_numbers)} response numbers grounded in tool output.",
    )

How it works:

  1. extract_numbers() uses two passes to find all numbers in a string. Pass 1 uses regex on the full text to catch thousand-separated numbers like $1,799.46. Pass 2 splits by commas first, then extracts from each field - this handles CSV data where 4,129.99 is two fields (quantity 4 and price 129.99), not the number 4,129.99. Both passes normalize by removing commas.
  2. The scorer collects numbers from all tool outputs (what the tools returned, extracted from the trace's tool-role messages), then checks that the agent's response numbers overlap with the tool output numbers. If there is overlap, the response is grounded in real data.
  3. If the agent mentions numbers that have zero overlap with any tool output, it is flagged as a possible hallucination. The agent may also compute derived values (percentages, differences) that do not appear in tool output - these are valid math and do not trigger the check.

The dataset (eval/dataset.py) stays the same - the groundedness scorer works with the existing test cases.

Now update eval/evaluate.py (path from project root) to include the new scorer. The only change from Step 4 is the import line and the scorers list - the setup block stays the same:

Run the evaluation again from the project root (first-react-agent/) :

uv run python -m eval.evaluate

When it finishes, copy the URL from the terminal output and open it in your browser. You will now see two scorer columns - groundedness and tool_selection - for each evaluation trace. Your latest run should show 1 (100%) for both scorers in the left panel. In the right panel, the evaluation traces show "true" for both scorers, while the autolog duplicate traces show null.

A note on edge cases: The extract_numbers function handles the most common formats (integers, decimals, thousand-separated, and CSV data). It uses two passes - one for prose and one for CSV fields - so it correctly handles both "$1,799.46" in a sentence and "4,129.99" as two CSV columns. It will not handle percentages, scientific notation, or other unusual formats. Start with this, iterate when you see false positives or negatives in your specific use case.

Step 6: Scope Guard - Does It Stay in Its Lane?

Theory

Your agent's system prompt says "If the user asks something unrelated to sales data, politely decline." But does it actually do this?

A scope guard verifies two things when the agent receives an off-topic question:

  1. Zero tool calls - the agent should not fetch any data for questions outside its scope
  2. Decline language - the response should contain polite decline phrasing

Why does this matter? If your agent confidently answers "the weather in Berlin is 22 degrees" - despite having no weather data - users will question what else it makes up. An agent that admits its boundaries builds more trust than one that pretends to know everything.

This is another deterministic check. No LLM judge needed - you check for zero tool calls and look for decline phrases.

Why this matters before production: In production, users will ask your agent all kinds of unexpected questions. If the agent goes off-scope even once, it undermines trust in every other answer. A scope guard ensures the agent consistently stays within its boundaries. It also catches a common failure mode after prompt changes - sometimes editing the system prompt to improve one behavior accidentally removes the decline instruction.

Lab

Open eval/scorers.py (path from project root) and add the scope_guard scorer. Here is the complete file with all three scorers:

"""Evaluation scorers for the sales agent."""

import re

from mlflow.entities import Feedback
from mlflow.genai.scorers import scorer


def extract_numbers(text: str) -> set[str]:
    """Extract all numbers from text, including decimals and comma-separated."""
    numbers = set()
    text = str(text)
    # Pass 1: treat commas as thousand separators (e.g., "$1,799.46")
    for match in re.findall(r"\d[\d,]*\.\d+|\d[\d,]*", text):
        numbers.add(match.replace(",", ""))
    # Pass 2: split by commas first, then extract (handles CSV like "4,129.99")
    for field in text.replace("\n", ",").split(","):
        for match in re.findall(r"\d+\.\d+|\d+", field.strip()):
            numbers.add(match)
    return numbers


def get_tool_calls(trace):
    """Extract tool names from assistant messages in the trace."""
    tools = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "assistant":
                    for tc in msg.get("tool_calls", []):
                        name = tc.get("function", {}).get("name")
                        if name:
                            tools.append(name)
    return tools


def get_tool_outputs(trace):
    """Extract tool output text from tool messages in the trace."""
    outputs = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "tool":
                    outputs.append(msg.get("content", ""))
    return outputs


@scorer
def tool_selection(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check that the agent called the expected tools."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # If no tools expected (e.g., off-topic question), check none were called
    if not expected_tools:
        if not actual_tools:
            return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly called no tools.")
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Expected no tool calls, but called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check each expected tool was called
    missing = [t for t in expected_tools if t not in actual_tools]
    if missing:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Missing tools: {missing}. Called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    return Feedback(value=True, rationale=f"All expected tools called: {actual_tools}")


@scorer
def groundedness(inputs, outputs, trace):
    """Response numbers should come from tool output, not be hallucinated."""
    tool_outputs = get_tool_outputs(trace)

    if not tool_outputs:
        # No tools called - nothing to ground against
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No tool calls to check against.")

    # Collect all numbers from tool outputs
    tool_numbers = set()
    for output in tool_outputs:
        tool_numbers.update(extract_numbers(output))

    # Extract numbers from the agent's response
    response_numbers = extract_numbers(str(outputs))

    if not response_numbers:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No numbers in response to verify.")

    # Check how many response numbers are grounded in tool output
    grounded = response_numbers & tool_numbers

    if not grounded:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale="No response numbers found in tool output - possible hallucination.",
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"{len(grounded)} of {len(response_numbers)} response numbers grounded in tool output.",
    )


@scorer
def scope_guard(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Off-topic questions should get zero tool calls and a polite decline."""
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # This scorer only applies to off-topic scenarios (expected_tools is empty)
    if expected_tools:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Not an off-topic scenario, skipping.")

    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)

    # Check 1: No tools should be called
    if actual_tools:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Called tools for off-topic question: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check 2: Response should contain decline language
    response_lower = str(outputs).lower()
    decline_phrases = [
        "can only",
        "only assist",
        "only help",
        "sales",
        "cannot help",
        "not able to",
        "outside",
        "beyond my",
        "don't have",
        "do not have",
    ]

    if any(phrase in response_lower for phrase in decline_phrases):
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly declined off-topic question.")

    return Feedback(
        value=False,
        rationale=(
            f"No decline language found in response. "
            f"Expected phrases like 'can only assist with sales'. "
            f"Got: {str(outputs)[:200]}"
        ),
    )

Now update eval/dataset.py (path from project root) to add a second off-topic scenario. The three on-topic scenarios stay the same, and we add "Write me a poem about cats" to test creative off-topic requests alongside the weather question from Step 4. Here is the complete file:

"""Golden trajectory dataset for the sales agent."""

SALES_AGENT_DATASET = [
    # On-topic: should call tools
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "How were sales in January?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
        },
    },
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Give me a complete summary of March sales"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
        },
    },
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "What were sales in February 2025?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
        },
    },
    # Off-topic: should decline
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "What's the weather in Berlin?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": [],
        },
    },
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Write me a poem about cats"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": [],
        },
    },
]

Now update eval/evaluate.py (path from project root) to include the new scorer. Same setup as before - only the import and scorers list change:

"""Run evaluation suite for the sales agent."""

import os
os.environ["MLFLOW_ENABLED"] = "false"

import mlflow
from uuid import uuid4

from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig
from first_react_agent.agent import create_app
from eval.scorers import tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard  # added scope_guard
from eval.dataset import SALES_AGENT_DATASET

# Setup (same as Step 4 - see explanation there)
config = AICoreConfig()
mlflow.set_tracking_uri(config.mlflow_tracking_uri)
mlflow.set_experiment(config.mlflow_experiment_name)

agent = create_app()

mlflow.langchain.autolog(log_traces=True, run_tracer_inline=True)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(disable=True)


def predict_fn(question: str) -> str:
    """Run the agent and return the final response text."""
    result = agent.invoke(
        {"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": question}]},
        {"configurable": {"thread_id": str(uuid4())}},
    )
    return result["messages"][-1].content


results = mlflow.genai.evaluate(
    data=SALES_AGENT_DATASET,
    predict_fn=predict_fn,
    scorers=[tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard],       # added scope_guard
)

print(results.tables["eval_results"])

Run from the project root (first-react-agent/) :

uv run python -m eval.evaluate

When it finishes, copy the URL from the terminal output and open it in your browser. You now have three scorers checking every scenario. Your latest run should show 1 (100%) for all three scorers - scope_guard, tool_selection, and groundedness - in the left panel. In the right panel, the evaluation traces show "true" for all three scorers, while the autolog duplicates show null.

Layer 3: End-to-End Trajectories - Now that you have individual scorers, Step 7 combines them into complete scenario tests - your agent's regression test suite.

Step 7: Golden Trajectories - The Full Test Suite

Theory

So far, we have built individual scorers - each one checking a single dimension (tool selection, groundedness, scope). A golden trajectory brings them all together into a complete scenario test. For a given question, it defines:

  1. Input: The user's question
  2. Expected tools: Which tools should be called
  3. Expected parameters: What arguments should be passed (optional, for critical scenarios)
  4. Constraints: Max tool calls allowed, must converge within a reasonable number of steps

Think of golden trajectories as your agent's regression test suite. Every time you change the system prompt, update tools, switch models, or modify configuration, you run the golden trajectories to verify nothing broke. If a scenario that used to pass starts failing, you have found a regression before it reaches users.

Why golden trajectories matter for production:

Before production, golden trajectories answer: "Is this agent ready to ship?" You define the behaviors you care about (correct tools, grounded numbers, staying in scope, efficiency), encode them as test cases, and gate every deployment on them passing. No green suite, no deploy.

In production, golden trajectories answer: "Is this agent still working?" LLM providers update models over time. A model that handled "Compare January and February" perfectly last month might fail after an update. Running golden trajectories on a nightly schedule catches this drift before users report issues.

How to design golden trajectories:

Scenario type What it tests Example
Happy pathAgent handles the core use case correctly"How were sales in January?"
Multi-toolAgent chains tools in the right order"Summarize March sales" (fetch then calculate)
Parameter accuracyAgent passes correct arguments"What were sales in February 2025?" (month + year)
Edge caseAgent handles unusual but valid inputs"Compare January and February"
Off-topicAgent declines gracefully"What's the weather?"
Adjacent topicAgent does not get confused by related but unsupported questions"How do I configure SAP?"

Start with 5-10 scenarios covering the most important behaviors. You can always add more as you discover new edge cases in production.

Lab

Let's expand the dataset to a full golden trajectory suite and add an efficiency scorer.

"""Golden trajectory dataset for the sales agent."""

SALES_AGENT_DATASET = [
    # S01: Basic query - fetch and summarize
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "How were sales in January?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
            "max_tool_calls": 4,
        },
    },
    # S02: Explicit summary request
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Give me a complete summary of March sales"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
            "max_tool_calls": 4,
        },
    },
    # S03: Specific month and year
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "What were sales in February 2025?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
            "max_tool_calls": 4,
        },
    },
    # S04: Off-topic - weather
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "What's the weather in Berlin?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": [],
            "max_tool_calls": 0,
        },
    },
    # S05: Off-topic - creative
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Write me a poem about cats"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": [],
            "max_tool_calls": 0,
        },
    },
    # S06: Multi-month comparison
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Compare January and February sales"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
            "max_tool_calls": 6,

What changed from Step 6's dataset: Every scenario now has a max_tool_calls field (used by the new efficiency scorer). We also added scenario S06 (multi-month comparison) which requires more tool calls and tests the agent's ability to handle complex queries. Each scenario now has an ID comment (# S01, # S02, etc.) for easy reference.

Now add an efficiency scorer to eval/scorers.py (path from project root). Here is the complete file with all four scorers:

"""Evaluation scorers for the sales agent."""

import re

from mlflow.entities import Feedback
from mlflow.genai.scorers import scorer


def extract_numbers(text: str) -> set[str]:
    """Extract all numbers from text, including decimals and comma-separated."""
    numbers = set()
    text = str(text)
    # Pass 1: treat commas as thousand separators (e.g., "$1,799.46")
    for match in re.findall(r"\d[\d,]*\.\d+|\d[\d,]*", text):
        numbers.add(match.replace(",", ""))
    # Pass 2: split by commas first, then extract (handles CSV like "4,129.99")
    for field in text.replace("\n", ",").split(","):
        for match in re.findall(r"\d+\.\d+|\d+", field.strip()):
            numbers.add(match)
    return numbers


def get_tool_calls(trace):
    """Extract tool names from assistant messages in the trace."""
    tools = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "assistant":
                    for tc in msg.get("tool_calls", []):
                        name = tc.get("function", {}).get("name")
                        if name:
                            tools.append(name)
    return tools


def get_tool_outputs(trace):
    """Extract tool output text from tool messages in the trace."""
    outputs = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "tool":
                    outputs.append(msg.get("content", ""))
    return outputs


@scorer
def tool_selection(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check that the agent called the expected tools."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # If no tools expected (e.g., off-topic question), check none were called
    if not expected_tools:
        if not actual_tools:
            return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly called no tools.")
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Expected no tool calls, but called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check each expected tool was called
    missing = [t for t in expected_tools if t not in actual_tools]
    if missing:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Missing tools: {missing}. Called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    return Feedback(value=True, rationale=f"All expected tools called: {actual_tools}")


@scorer
def groundedness(inputs, outputs, trace):
    """Response numbers should come from tool output, not be hallucinated."""
    tool_outputs = get_tool_outputs(trace)

    if not tool_outputs:
        # No tools called - nothing to ground against
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No tool calls to check against.")

    # Collect all numbers from tool outputs
    tool_numbers = set()
    for output in tool_outputs:
        tool_numbers.update(extract_numbers(output))

    # Extract numbers from the agent's response
    response_numbers = extract_numbers(str(outputs))

    if not response_numbers:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No numbers in response to verify.")

    # Check how many response numbers are grounded in tool output
    grounded = response_numbers & tool_numbers

    if not grounded:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale="No response numbers found in tool output - possible hallucination.",
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"{len(grounded)} of {len(response_numbers)} response numbers grounded in tool output.",
    )


@scorer
def scope_guard(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Off-topic questions should get zero tool calls and a polite decline."""
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # This scorer only applies to off-topic scenarios (expected_tools is empty)
    if expected_tools:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Not an off-topic scenario, skipping.")

    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)

    # Check 1: No tools should be called
    if actual_tools:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Called tools for off-topic question: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check 2: Response should contain decline language
    response_lower = str(outputs).lower()
    decline_phrases = [
        "can only",
        "only assist",
        "only help",
        "sales",
        "cannot help",
        "not able to",
        "outside",
        "beyond my",
        "don't have",
        "do not have",
    ]

    if any(phrase in response_lower for phrase in decline_phrases):
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly declined off-topic question.")

    return Feedback(
        value=False,
        rationale=(
            f"No decline language found in response. "
            f"Expected phrases like 'can only assist with sales'. "
            f"Got: {str(outputs)[:200]}"
        ),
    )


@scorer
def efficiency(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check the agent did not loop or make excessive calls."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    max_calls = expectations.get("max_tool_calls", 4)

    # Check total tool calls are not excessive
    if len(actual_tools) > max_calls:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=(
                f"Too many tool calls: {len(actual_tools)} "
                f"(expected {max_calls} or fewer)"
            ),
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"Efficient: {len(actual_tools)} tool call(s) within limit of {max_calls}.",
    )

Did you know? MLflow provides a built-in ToolCallEfficiency scorer that checks for redundant tool calls using an LLM judge. Our custom efficiency scorer above is deterministic (no LLM call, free, instant), which makes it better for CI. But if you want a quick start without writing custom code, ToolCallEfficiency() from mlflow.genai.scorers works out of the box.

Now update eval/evaluate.py (path from project root) with all four scorers. Same setup - only the import and scorers list change:

from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig
from first_react_agent.agent import create_app
from eval.scorers import tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard, efficiency  # added efficiency
from eval.dataset import SALES_AGENT_DATASET

# Setup (same as Step 4 - see explanation there)
config = AICoreConfig()
mlflow.set_tracking_uri(config.mlflow_tracking_uri)
mlflow.set_experiment(config.mlflow_experiment_name)

agent = create_app()

mlflow.langchain.autolog(log_traces=True, run_tracer_inline=True)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(disable=True)


def predict_fn(question: str) -> str:
    """Run the agent and return the final response text."""
    result = agent.invoke(
        {"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": question}]},
        {"configurable": {"thread_id": str(uuid4())}},

    return result["messages"][-1].content


results = mlflow.genai.evaluate(
